content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (2000) is such a piece of art that everyone should watch atleast once in their lifetime.

**Score:** 129
**Body:** This movie is honestly a masterpiece that everyone should watch at least once in their life. I love it so much that I end up watching it again every year.

The movie is blend of action, romance, and drama. The romance, without any obscenity, is outstanding. By the end of the movie, it can easily make you emotional and even bring tears to your eyes. The cinematography is also amazing and makes many scenes feel special.

It’s also a great watch for action fans. I personally like action movies, and this one truly impressed me. The choreography and the way weapons are used make the action scenes very engaging. The movie has many action sequences that are really enjoyable.

Every character has a meaningful story and that’s what turns this movie into such a memorable experience.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorites for sure.
  - Do you have watched other Chinese movies? I love Chinese classics, like Hero, House of flying daggers, Once upon a time in china, etc. 
Journey to the west really surprised me and it's climax which is face off of Monkey King and Buddha is pure masterpiece. 
    - I’ll check out Journey to the west. I’d recommend fist of Legend with Jet Li. If you haven’t seen “Kung Fu Hustle”. Stop what you are doing immediately and watch that.
2. Ang Lee had such a crazy run in that time period. Ice Storm is one of my favorite movies from that period and I wish it got a little more love. Crouching Tiger is such a love letter to Chinese folklore and cinema and I feel like it’s a must watch for lovers of film. Ideally on the big screen.
3. I enjoyed it but I've spoken to so many people who found it boring, incomprehensible, or simply couldn't suspend their disbelief for all the flying leaps. It's an incredible film if you are familiar with the cinematic traditions it's drawing from.
  - True. Even my brother didn’t like the movie because of the floating effects and martial arts action, but I found it absolutely amazing. It actually starts off slow and simple, but keeps getting better with every passing minute.


4. I remember seeing this in a theater and just being absolutely dumbstruck afterwards.  Totally speechless.  One the most magical theater experiences of my lifetime.
5. One of my favorite movies,beautifully shot and great soundtrack.
6. I would add that it should be watched in the original language, the dubbed version takes you right out of the mood of the film.
7. Yes, it is. What is amazing is the way the two main female characters are written and portrayed. The elder woman who inspite of being in love with Li Mu Bai respects tradition and restrains her feelings whereas on the other hand the younger woman rebels and goes ahead with her instinct.

Ang Lee made a masterpiece.

## 2. Movies that were like conspiracy theories l, but are now looking more realistic

**Score:** 31
**Body:** IDK HOW TO EDIT THE TITLE I'M SORRY FOR THE TYPOS

In connection to the Epstein files and all that stuff. Any movies that were exposing secrets thru art is the kind of vibe I'm looking for. 
It can be realistic or crazy, any where in the spectrum will do. Anything really that reveals what's behind the scenes. Exposing different industries. 

Thank you for all the recommendations, I want to try each one!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eyes Wide Shut (1999)
2. Wag The Dog is very very pertinent to today. Considering the central operation of the plot is literally about to happen again any day now with Iran and the Epstein Files.

Also Enemy of the State is a movie in which all of the mass surveillance technology seemed almost like science fiction and flight of fancy, until Edward Snowden risked life and limb to prove that it was all real. In retrospect it just seems like an advertisement of the capabilities of the NSA while they were still a secret.
3. under the silver lake actor is aandrew Garfield.. it gives u weird experience and it has same temple designs which epstiens island temple had
  - Yeah ,if Epstien is alive ,this movie clearly explains his whereabouts
4. I thought Idiocracy (2006) was a comedy when I first watched it.. but I think it’s definitely happening / happened. The horror.
5. Eyes Wide Shut (1999)
6. Try The Conspiracy.
7. You Were Never Really Here

The Manchurian Candidate 

The Parallax View

Blow Out
8. Get Him to the Greek (2010) 

Jonah Hill. Russell Brand. Diddy. Each playing a character that turned out to be exactly who they were.
9. The Insider (1999) has that dark conspiracy thriller vibe, it’s about the inner workings of the tobacco industry and the pressure a whistleblower comes under. It’s very good

**Score:** 29

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dolores Claiborne (1995)
2. Malice. You’ll really like it. Good twists.
3. Scarlet Street (1945)

Rope (1948)

Eyes Wide Shut (1999)

4. Wind River 

The Prestige 

No Country for Old Men 

Nocturnal Animals
5. The conversation
6. I assume you’ve seen Misery (1990) but adding it here just incase. Also adding I See You (2019)
7. Watch Strange Darling, Green Room, and Blue Ruin!
8. Body heat
9. Fallen
10. We Need to Talk About Kevin

## 4. The Game (1997)

**Score:** 28
**Body:** If you’re looking for a movie where every decision matters and the tension slowly builds, The Game (1997) is one I always recommend. It starts slow but turns into a psychological puzzle where you constantly question what’s real and what isn’t. Great if you want something immersive that pulls you in without needing explosions or nonstop action.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is one of my favorite Michael Douglas films. Highly recommend.
2. I literally am always recommending this film to people online or otherwise. Great movie. 10/10 for me.
  - Honestly same. It’s one of those movies where you keep questioning everything the whole time. I love films that mess with your head without needing nonstop action.
    - It’s one of those movies I wish I can watch for the first time again. Ending was great 🤌🏿
3. That’s one of my all time favorite movies! It is incredible!!
4. The audience is as confused as Nicholas is. Great, great movie!
5. “Right now, I am very fragile.”
6. I’ve seen this movie about 25 times. lol! It’s great!!
7. Everytime this movie is recommended. You just lost the game.
8. If Fincher would have stuck the landing this would be a 5/5 movie

## 5. Favorite classic films with minimal sexism?

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I'd really like to watch more classic films, but I'm currently in the mood to do so without being reminded of the strict gender roles of the past. 

For an example, I adore It's a Wonderful Life, but I can't watch it without being somewhat uncomfortable with some of the scenes (Mary in the bush, her horrifying old maid alternate universe, etc.). I know it has to be viewed in context, the way courtship worked back then, the ways it was progressive in its depiction of Mary for the time, and all that jazz. I also know it's good to be made uncomfortable sometimes by film and not simply watch what's "safe". 

But right now, I just want some films where I can simply enjoy them without being made painfully aware of the way women were generally viewed at the time. Bonus if there's films with good female representation, especially if it was subversive for the time, but I'd also accept any that sidestep gender altogether, like 12 Angry Men.

Thank you!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His Girl Friday. In spite of the title.
2. Arsenic and Old Lace
3. I suggest the two to three Mae West movies she made pre-code. Not only were they written by a woman (West), and it stars her in a very sexual liberated role. She’s sexy and wise cracking and a total girls girl. Extremely feminist for the time. Also Cary Grant is in at least two of them, and he’s very nice to look at lol
4. Ninotchka, such a great film and Greta Garbo doesn’t take crap from anyone.
5. I’m not sure if this will fit, but His Girl Friday.

Definitely a great film, the leads in the original play, The Front Page were changed from two men to a man and a woman. So it’s gendered but also subverts gender stereotypes, certainly of the time and perhaps contemporary ones as well.
  - The one thing that bugs me about this is that her ex husband is seen as doing the right thing by manipulating her the way he does. Like he knows better than she does how she wants to live her life.
    - To an extent, but I don’t know that this is presented as gendered “I know what’s best for you” as a regard for his ex’s talent as a journalist. Walter  shown to be a horrible, if occasionally charming, human being who cares only about the newspaper. So, naturally, he would try to get Hildy to stay in the newspaper trade.

Again, not sure if His Girl Friday is the break from a gendered movies that OP asked for, but it definitely subverts a lot of gender roles.
6. Auntie Maime


7. Trouble in Paradise (1932) is one of my favorites. It’s also a Lubitsch, which someone else recommended.
8. The Women (1939)  An all female cast

Now, Voyager (1942)

The Ghost & Mrs. Muir (1947)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. ‘American Psycho’ Author Bret Easton Ellis Says ‘High Profile Actors’ Have Turned Down Patrick Bateman Role: ‘They Don’t Want To Be in the Shoes of Christian Bale’

**Score:** 757

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dicaprio was signed on to do it before Bale, and I try to picture that film with Leo and just cant. Bale crushed it. Bale would lose out to him on several roles eventually and the 2 had a bit of a quiet rivalry. Now theyve both been cast in HEAT 2 and I couldn't be happier.
  - Bale's said in interviews that every role he ever got his whole career was because Leo passed on it.
    - Leonardo DiCaprio was gonna be in Newsies?
      - Yeah maybe he was being a bit hyperbolic. Might not have worked for empire of the sun either.
  - I believe the production was original Mary Harron directing with Christian Bale in the lead. Then the production let them go because they wanted bigger names. They signed Oliver Stone to direct it and he tried to get Dicaprio but he passed. After Leo passed then Stone dropped out and the production went back to Harron and Bale.
2. If we're going to get another Bret Easton Ellis adaptation, I'd like to see someone take a swing at Glamorama.
  - How about a real “Less than Zero” and not a 80’s “don’t do drugs PSA” with good music and Robert Downey Jr. 

But Glamorama would be cool.
3. It’s a BIG ask! He was very good in that part. Let’s stop remaking already well rendered adaptations.
4. take the hint bret, adapt a different story! this one is done!
5. There are certain roles and movies that just can’t be replicated. Bale as Bateman is one of them. If it’s remade at best the actor will be perceived as “meh they’re alright I guess but they lack the [insert whatever it is they didn’t do as well] that Bale had” OR the more likely critique will be that they’re a far cry and pale impersonation of the character Bale dynamically brought to life

## 2. Even though it was yesterday, Happy birthday to Jennifer Love Hewitt!! Do you have a favorite character she played?

**Score:** 346
**Body:** I’ll start. Dr. Liz Wilson in the Garfield movies 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can't Hardly Wait-Amanda!
  - Aman-Duh!!!!
    - Wow Mike, you really got me there… 

*walks aways*
  - ![gif](giphy|HdlkwA7RsT1sc)
2. Heartbreakers, but Ghost Whisperer was fun too so honorable mention.
3. I haven't seen her in many things, but she and Sigourney Weaver made a great team in *Heartbreakers*.
  - Yeah that movie was a revelation for me, having not seen Galaxy Quest yet.
4. Jennifer Love Fefferman, *Boy Meets World* (Season 5, Episode 17 *And then there was Shawn*)
5. I was a kid in the 90's and 00's. So I'll NEVER NOT love her. And not sure I have a FAVORITE. Any time she shows up though, I will stop and admire her.
6. I had a huge crush on her when I was a little girl. I’m a sucker for horror movies and I remember her being in I  Know What You Did Last Summer. Bad movies overall but I was a kid and I didn’t care.

## 3. What do you think of Hot Tub Time Machine (2010)? Does it hold up or was it never funny to begin with?

**Score:** 290

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. When Craig Robinson called his wife when she was a kid, lmao that was funny
  - Hang. Up. The. Phone. Jerry. This doesn’t concern you.
    - THIS IS BETWEEN ME AND MY WIFE!!!!
  - ADMIT IT! YOU SUCKED HIS BALLS, YOU LICKED HIS BOOTY!

The girl on the other end: 😳
2. I think it’s fucking hilarious and way underappreciated, particularly in a world where people are bemoaning a lack of comedies nowadays. Rob Cordry’s performance is far better than it needed to be and I love a film where there are really zero “good,” people, you’re just watching some jackasses who broadly mean no harm fuck around. Craig Robinson’s immediate instinct upon finding out they travelled through time is to start snorting cocaine, that’s fucking funny. They’re not good guys, they give up on Back to the Futuring almost immediately and are petty, selfish and god damn hilarious.
  - This is my take. 

There’s not many good comedies these days and the few that are good tend to be comedy adjacent like they’re really a thriller but there’s black comedy elements in it. 

This doesn’t ever pretend to be anything more than it is. And it’s legitimately funny.
  - Lougle and motley Lou will always be funny to me.
3. Loved it then and now
4. Far exceeded my expectations. Watched it again a few years later, and still enjoyed myself. But… it absolutely did not need a sequel.
5. It reminds me a lot of ace ventura pet detective. It’s hilarious to people who were at the right age when it came out. I still find it hilarious, but my dad hates it

Edit: credit to rewatchables for part of my take

## 4. Are there films other than Fitzcarraldo where a first-of-its-kind human achievement was captured on film?

**Score:** 221

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. IIRC, back when practical effects were king, a number of James Bond movies got into the Guinness Book Of World Records: stuff like the speedboat jump in Live And Let Die being longer than any ever done before, sure, but also stuff like the mid-air upside-down barrel roll in The Man With The Golden Gun getting “credited with being the first stunt ever to be calculated by computer modeling”.
  - You recall correctly! Geeky kid me loved the Guinness Book and read it obsessively.
2. The Moon Landing by Kubrick is a great one! 
  - I’m kidding :( 
  - I love the idea that Kubrick arranged a real moon landing ala Fitzcarraldo and the real conspiracy is just that NASA took the credit.
    - There's that old joke that Kubrick was such a perfectionist that he insisted that they film on location.
3. Bridge over the river Kwai, they built and subsequently blew up a real bridge for the movie.
4. 1492: Conquest of Paradise - setting up the first colony in America, perhaps? If you liked to watch the steamboat being hauled, surely you will love the sequence with the church bell being pulled up atop the belfry.
5. True  offered to kill klaus      there is a doc film about the production
6. You definitely need to watch The Claim (2000). There are many parallels between that film and this, its worth a deep dive ontop of watching the film. Good luck!

## 5. What is your opinion on this second adaptation of Elmore Leonard’s short story “Three-Ten to Yuma”?

**Score:** 212
**Body:** Bale and Russell were at the top of their game, and the storyline was strong, with beautiful cinematography. What’s your opinion on it?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “You boys, posse?  I hate posses.”

Ben Foster is amazing in this film.
  - To be fair, he is brilliant in everything I’ve seen him in
    - Yup, hell and high water, hostage, alpha dog. All amazing.
      - You know what that makes me…? Comanche
  - “WE’RE GOING TO CONTENTION.”
2. Still a good movie. I went through a Crowe binge recently and this one stood out. Solid western.
3. Very solid, with great performances all around. 

Ben Foster is *incredibleeeeeeee* in this movie.
4. One of my favorites easily. But it would be hard to mess something up with Crowe and Bale together. And the Dynamic between the 2 characters is special. Great movie.
5. recently rewatched and its very entertaining thanks to the great performances
6. Second best western in the last 30 years

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Kill Bill is a metaphor for grooming

**Score:** 477
**Body:** I have just rewatched Kill Bill 1 and 2 back to back and I feel that the films can be viewed as someone growing out of a grooming relationship. 

The Bride clearly had lingering feelings for Bill when she was rehearsing ber wedding, which is common for people who were groomed. She even called him "daddy" as a double entendre before heading to the aisle. 

She was literally named Kiddo but we were led to believe that Bill was calling her this as a pet name (which also could be a nod to shows like SATC, which were booming at the time, and where the main relationship was between an older man calling his girlfriend in her thirties "kid"). 

Also, Beatrix is framed as "special", Bill has a sweet side that only she knows, Beatrix is the most lacking in backstory and development, she has no past unlike O-Ren Ishii and no future - not until she kills Bill. 

Beatrix is the only one that is not allowed to leave Bill peacefully, all the other Vipers go and do their own thing after the hit at the chapel. 

Their thing is better (O-Ren) or worse (Budd), but it is absolutely theirs. Beatrix does not even have a name until the last half of the second movie. She "roars and rampages" in the film's present, but in the past she was mesmerized by Bill and docile. He treated her terribly and she was all smiles and ready for more. 

She was a "natural-born killer" because he told her that she was, just like he was trying to instill in their daughter. Who was she before? No one. Because Bill made sure that she was no one. 

She only gets her oneness through confronting her groomer - and before that, taking apart the world he had created. Interestingly, Elle, who seems like the biggest Bill devout, turns around and kills Pai Mei. 

She was enamored with Bill, but she wasn't as compliant, she was still her own person; Elle probably despises Beatrix because she is the "favorite", the one who was successfully groomed to be Bill's partner, but being a favorite also means being a puppet until you break your strings violently. 

  
Just needed to get these thoughts out, valuable or not. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. We learn that Bill grew up with a Pimp in Mexico whom he has seen as a father. From him I guess he learned how to manipulate vulnerable women, make them fall in love with him and make them do anything for him - including killing people.
  - I really appreciated that scene; particularly the way Esteban is supposedly being all charming and avuncular and then gives that line “he shot you in the head??? I would have been so much nicer, I would have just cut your face,” which is timed out like a joke, but then one of his girls brings them tequila and we see her face is cut and he grimaces at her then has her wipe her spit up with his hanky and she *thanks* him. 

Just before we meet Bill, and all of his supposed charm; its like the final sign at the gate “abandon all hope ye who enter” that these are men who will control and hurt women around them without conscience or remorse, or honor and humanity, whatever the case may be.

Edit: well, hey, just saw an OC posted earlier to this that actually outlines this but with deeper analysis.
    - My personal head cannon is Beatrix kills Esteban. What a disgusting old man.
2. You can even see how Bill treats Elle Driver in a creepy way: like any woman he is involved with is under his thrall. I wouldn't say this is even a metaphor. Bill \*did\* groom Beatrix, he's a controlling bastard. That's why he's the leader of the vipers
  - Yeah what’s the metaphor? It’s the plot.
  - Code Name: The Snake Charmer
  - The very first shot of Bill in Part 1 is when he calls Elle on the phone to cancel the assassination he ordered, and all you see of him is [his hand stroking and manipulating the phallic handle of his katana. (It has a demon on it too.)](https://youtu.be/ts4gADntnOY?si=9zpz0npQ7tCC-qkW&t=68)
3. I don't think Kill Bill is necessarily a metaphor for grooming more than grooming is just implied within the narrative and an ancillary fuel to the firey revenge. That's just my opinion, though.

I like that you brought it up, because I haven't actually seen a discussion about this before. The analysis in general is really cool and I hadn't thought of a lot of the stuff you did.

Nice post, OP!
4. I really like this read on the film. We know that their relationship was built on violence and an imbalance of power and there are those awkward sexual tension vibes an a way that almost feels taboo when they interact. A lot is being left unsaid but I think you have a really interesting take and I wonder how heavily Tarantino meant to imply this. It does seem subtle yet deliberate
5. I'm grateful to this post because it gives me a different way to think about one of the most important scenes in the films, Beatrix's meeting with Esteban the pimp. Esteban serves as a foil to Bill because he belongs to a different genre than the rest of the film, with a more grounded depiction of misogynistic violence. Rather than killing Beatrix in revenge for running away, he says he "would have just cut your face"—something we immediately see he's done to one of the women he traffics. 

In the past, I've interpreted this scene as clarifying the ethics Bill, Beatrix, and the other characters (even Elle) live by in their heightened revenge tragedy. They may all be murdering bastards, they may all be cruel and duplicitous, they may leave orphans in their wake. But even so, they respect each other at some base human level as worthy adversaries. In Tarantino's universe, it may well be less of an ethical breach to massacre your ex and her wedding guests because she dumped you than it is to mutilate a woman's face to disempower and humiliate her. (And I could say a lot more about the way Tarantino finds contextual justifications for extreme violence against his female characters as not “just” misogynistic, especially in his last two movies…)

Considering your analysis, though, maybe Bill has more in common with Esteban than I thought. And maybe Beatrix has more in common with Esteban's victim, bearing internal scars from being reshaped in Bill's image and having her future family stolen by him. 

## 2. If I had legs I’d kick you and the critique of the western therapy model

**Score:** 122
**Body:** So many layers to this film. I don’t normally post thoughts on here but I’d like to share this one to ask if anyone else felt this strongly.

Most of the reviews about this film comment on the difficulties of motherhood and “bad therapists” but I found the central point of this film was to critique modern therapy model and the way we use it.

No amount of breathing exercises will pause the relentlessness or unpredictability of life. The Western therapy industrial complex is a symptom of a society that has outsourced family and communal care (and the responsibility that comes with it) to paid professionals. 

We see this in the protagonist‘s therapy room. The spectrum goes from surface level chats to one client leaving their baby with her. Therapists have now taken the role of friend and caretaker. This is even shown with her and her own therapeutic supervisor.

Character’s in this film have deeply unsupportive and cruel partners. And having an hour in therapy each week is probably the only time they talk about it. Same goes with the support group at the hospital for sick children. We witness and blunt and robotic healthcare professional talk about “blame and shame”. While I understand not everyone has a good support system, there has to be a better way than the individualistic modern therapy approach.

ASAP Rocky’s character is the only person in this film who fulfils the community role in the film. He expects no thanks and he approaches it from a genuine desire to connect and help. “It takes a village”. 

Anyway, I hope I put that well. I really enjoyed this film and went in not really knowing what to expect. I rated 4 stars.

  


### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Note: I'm not educated in this, this is just my current laymen understanding:


I think it's worth noting that the type of therapy you're describing is specifically supportive psychotherapy, or supportive therapy, where the goal is what you are describing: somebody to lean on, validate, vent to, things of immediate concern. Ideally you would get to a point where you are able to address the things that lead to needing that support. Motivation, self esteem, trauma, behavioral patterns, whatever.
  - Yes and it’s used for that in most cases. But I think it is also now being used more than ever as a replacement for friends and community. 
    - >I think it is also now being used more than ever as a replacement for friends and community. 

Worth pointing out there's a lot of variation in the support provided by friends and community - if that's even available at all. 

If you're part of a religious community or family, some of the "support" can be downright harmful.

Still therapy is in a bad place, right now.

In my personal experience, just getting therapy is almost impossible - especially for children.
    - I just want to point this out— I have a job with a NDA. There are parts of my life that I legally cannot talk to anyone about except someone with confidentiality.
2. >but I found the central point of this film was to critique modern therapy model and the way we use it.

I don't know if that's the "central point" of the film, just because it's not quite articulated what exact therapies were being used, and because I didn't feel like anyone in her sphere was actually interested in helping her. Even when they did, if that makes any sense, it felt like the point of the film is that she was so difficult to help at all.

And that's not to blame her, or those in her sphere, at all... it's more about just how difficult it is from all ends. I don't see a lot of blame in this film, I just see a lot of acknowledgment of the complex horrors of modern life. 
3. And the film was about three layers deep in people who were in love with their therapists just because their therapists listen to them, even without offering actual help. I think you're giving too much benefit of the doubt to Rocky's character though - he doesn't give her an out at the end and quickly replaces her with another woman, so I don't think his attachment or offered attachment was genuinely that deep or altruistic. He says it himself, he was drawn in by the daughter screaming, not because he wanted to.
4. My wife and I watched this film, afterword we watched an episode of shrinking as a palate cleanser :)

Its an intense movie, once I realized that a Safdie (Uncut Gems etc) was involved it made sense - but I didn’t always feel the truth of it; as if it was performative display of stress and anxiety taken to 11

But all the actors (even Conan were great) and Rose Byrne really carried the ratcheting of tension nonstop

## 3. Which films is Sean baker talking about when he refers to sex comedies?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I saw Sean Baker mention that his next film is inspired by the Italian sex comedies of the 1960s and 1970s.

Does anyone know which specific movies he’s likely referring to? Is he talking about stuff like Divorce Italian Style, or more the “commedia sexy all’italiana” type films?

Would love some examples of the main titles or directors that define that era. Thanks!

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Like Carry On etc? Giovannona Long-Thigh? Confessions… series (1974–1977)?

The Core Titles

Giovannona Coscialunga disonorata con onore (1973)

One of the defining entries. Big farce energy. Edwige Fenech was the face of the genre.

L'insegnante (1975)

“The Schoolteacher.” Hugely influential. Spawned imitators for years.

La liceale (1975)

“The Schoolgirl.” Massive hit. Kicked off the “school” sub-cycle.

Quel gran pezzo dell'Ubalda tutta nuda e tutta calda (1972)

Medieval-set, absurd, bawdy.

La poliziotta (1974)

“The Policewoman.” Another recurring archetype.

La dottoressa del distretto militare (1976)

Military medical farce. Pure formula.
  - And this is just the tip of the iceberg. 

Anything with Edwige Fenech or Gloria Guida will likely meet the criteria
2. In my heart of hearts he's going for the absurd horny Tinto Brass kinda stuff lol

Not necessarily the Salon Kitty/Caligula-ish stuff but more the more grounded ones like Nerosubianco or La Vacanza

Probably more likely that he's going for more of a Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow/The Dolls/Malicious kinda thing

Then again maybe I'm wrong and we're getting sex cave women alla When Women Had Tails! That would be very funny and I'd personally really love it. Would be a really ballsy move.  
  
There's also a fun "comedic" sexy travelogue I watched last year where young Udo Kier played a gigolo picking up hot ladies on europe vacation and I think that would play well like a reverse Anora? Not sure folks would be into it.

Excited that anyone is playing around with the genre in a contemporary mode--I am eternally seduced by the combination of commedia and sexual and social politics.

## 4. Soundless Film Viewings: favoring the rewatch of good to great films with zero sound

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Some time ago, as a photographer, I did a study of class Film Noir by going through 30 or so classic films without sound. The idea then was that I wanted to focus in on the cinematic style, to feel the films just through image, because I was bringing [a Noir approach to my own photography in Thailand](http://8limbsus.com/muay-thai-forum/topic/1288-muay-noir-where-muay-thai-photography-and-film-noir-meet/). What surprised me was just how edifying the films were without sound, even those I wasn't familiar with at all. The construction of the film came to the surface in a way that was rich and full, and so many details rose to the surface of well known films without sound and dialogue directing my eyes. For a few years that study was a one-off and I didn't really follow up with other films. 

Then I got a big OLED screen and started devoting myself to more serious (re- and first time) watching. I became really entranced with Possession (1981) and after several watches I decided to give it a go without sound (in fact it wasn't without sound because I put on headphones and listened to a musical track from an artist I enjoy) and I was blown away by how much more of the film that came through for me. Tons of details I hadn't noticed before, but also a lot of emotional import, because I was seeing the physical acting and the faces differently. This film made an interesting case, as it has been argued - I later found out - that an interpretation of the film is that it is about the failure of Language. Żuławski was examining how language communication fails in relationships. What was compelling is that it lined up with my sense that the film is actually a Silent Film, and that the noted "overacting" and extreme physicality matches up with that kind of experience. A powerful layer of the meaning of the film was exposed in the soundless peel away. 

Since that time I've begun rewatching other films without sound. I tend to like cinematically potent film styles (from Tarkovsky to Cassavetes to Peckinpah) so each of them had a lot to give to a soundless viewing. This time without any added music in my ear. Just straight film on a big screen. When the film is well-made there is a tremendous amount of information in the frame that pulling away the sound opens up to our experience.

This leads to a larger thesis that I have about the nature of the art of Cinema. Cinema necessarily is the art of the moving image on a BIG screen. By big screen I mean a screen big enough that you can't take in the entire frame in a single glance. You as a viewer have to move your eyes and capture aspects of the frame at any one moment. In this way audience actually co-authors a film with the director, and one can never see the same film twice (because your eye falls and goes differently each time). The director is ever directing your eye in the frame, yes, but with a big enough screen the viewer's eye has tremendous individual freedom. The viewer's eye is in a sense "editing" the film's frame with its focus. This focus is often a focus of consciousness, but I believe that the actual muscular movement of the eyeball is part of the cinematic embodied experience. (This is why watching films on your phone, or films made for phone viewing in mind, or, many kinds of TV are not really "cinema" in my view.)

When you turn the sound off on movies, when before a big enough screen, the eye is given even MORE freedom to co-author the film. The director is deprived of several of the tools that is usually in her hand, both diegetic and non-diegetic sound, including most linguistic controls. The eye is free to wander through the frame much like one does in a large room art gallery before enormous oil paintings, and if the film is well made it can be intensely rewarding. 

I think also there is a sort of "pure cinema" type of experience when the moving image is allowed to carry the full load of the meaning of a film. At least for me as a very visual, photographic-minded person its very stimulating and inspiring. Scenes that otherwise might be quite plot or dialogue centric suddenly expand into specters of light, faint emotional eye performances, edits pop forward with space-time jumps, so much of the filmmaking comes forward.

Has anyone else had interesting experiences watching films without sound?

## 5. Vertigo (1958) and David Lynch

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Watching Vertigo again I got strong David Lynch vibes, and wouldn’t be surprised if the film inspired Lynch’s process.  
  
Take Twin Peaks, you’ve got a detective trying to solve a mundane murder mystery, but you end up plunging into deep subconscious dream realms that underpin reality itself, the whole thing is trippy as all hell.  
  
Similarly, in Vertigo our detective ‘Scottie’ is tasked with following a man’s wife, but she seems mad, maybe possessed, he falls in love and becomes obsessed with her, and when she ‘dies’ he loses his own mind and tries to transform a stranger into the object of his obsession.  
  
Gradually, the film *hypnotises* the viewer, just like a Lynch mystery, luring you in with a macguffin but actually plunging you into deep and dark parts of your own psyche. Images become increasingly abstract and the music swells into powerful haunting motifs, by the end you need some time to recover, as if you’ve just awoken from a pungent dream.  
  
Mulholland Drive especially seems to owe a debt to Vertigo, with brunettes turning blonde, shifting identities, actresses, obsessive love leading to madness, and repeated motifs. Then there’s the weird coincidence of David Lynch resembling ‘Jimmy Stewart from Mars’.  
  
It just seems like these two great directors were reaching for the same intangible thing at the core of the human psyche.
